data.title: Dividends Over Drama: Here's What Smart Money Is Buying
data.description:
Introduction

Have you ever heard of the cash trap?

Last year, I wrote a number of articles on this phenomenon, including one on October 30, which had the title "A $6.8 Trillion Cash Trap? 2 Of My Favorite Dividend Picks For What's Next."

Essentially, the cash trap is based on the fact that elevated short-term interest rates caused Americans to push trillions into money market funds, as they could get rates of 5% and more by simply holding risk-free government debt.

This was the result of the post-pandemic tightening cycle of the Federal Reserve, which made it much more attractive for many retirees to buy short-term government debt.

Moreover, despite some rate cuts last year, short-term debt is still attractive for many, as the chart from the New York Fed below shows.

With that said, the concept of the 'trap' was based on easing inflation and potentially longer-term rate cuts. These would have lowered money market fund rates and forced investors to look for income in places like the stock market.

For now, that hasn't happened, as the effective Federal Reserve rate is still north of 4.0%, a number a lot of people are perfectly comfortable with.

On the contrary.

These investments are not a trap but a total blessing!

As we all know by now, markets are in turmoil. Hence, it helps that "Americans Are Sitting on a Cash Pile as Stocks Reel," as The Wall Street Journal wrote in the title of a recent article.

As you may have guessed, the title refers to money market funds, which have ballooned to a stunning $7.4 trillion, $600 billion more than when I wrote the October article I just mentioned.

Moreover, to put things in perspective, Americans have close to a fifth of their assets in cash, as The Journal reports:

Some analysts say that Americans have simply shifted their assets into higher-yielding money markets from traditional bank accounts, and expect that cash to stay put. [...]

Cash is still paying higher yields than before the pandemic, even with the Fed’s rate cuts last year. The average return on money-market funds is at 4.2%, down from 4.3% in December, Crane data show. In March, individual investors raised the share of cash in their portfolios to about 18.3%, up from 17.4% the prior month, according to the American Association of Individual Investors. - WSJ (emphasis added)

For now, I agree with the article when it comes to expectations of this money staying put. As we can see below, 'nobody' is bullish, as AAII data shows that roughly 62% of all surveyed retail investors are bearish. That's twice the historical average.

The good news is that investors have an edge. While I doubt that anyone is enjoying this market carnage, the fact that most go into it with a decent cash position is fantastic news.

After all, who doesn't like buying the dip?

Here's What Smart Money Is Buying

In recent days, I have written a lot about the stocks that I am buying, and I will obviously continue to do so.

The problem is that I am currently (not in general) mainly buying dividend growth stocks with somewhat subdued yields, a strategy that works for me but not for many of my readers who require higher income.

So, as I promised, this article is a way to provide food for thought for a bigger audience.

In this article, I'm using some thoughts from 'smart money,' which were brought up in a Bloomberg article titled "What to Do With Your Money Right Now as Tariffs Reshape Markets."

As many opinions align with my own research, it provides a great framework for a wide range of investors.

For starters, a good start is to simply reassess one's portfolio. What do you own? Why did you buy it?

If you, like me, invested in long-term dividend stocks with a focus on resilient business models and a healthy balance sheet, odds are you don't have to make any (major) changes.

Especially investors who prefer index funds are in a great spot to do absolutely nothing. After all, one of the biggest factors that tends to derail our investment strategies is our consistent need to 'do something.'

After all, time in the market beats timing the market.

This doesn't just sound catchy, but it's also backed by decades worth of data.

As we can see in the chart below:

  • Since 1950, on a one-year rolling basis, stocks have been a casino. Nobody knows what the next 12 months look like. This includes billionaires, retail investors, and me.
  • On a 10-year rolling basis, the worst return for stock-only investors was basically flat.
  • Investors who invest in 60/40 portfolios have never lost money on a five-year rolling basis.
  • On a 20-year rolling basis, stock investors have never lost money.

It's worth noting that these numbers encompass the Great Financial Crisis, the pandemic, and every crash and rally since the end of the Second World War. That's quite a substantial data sample.

Having said that, as you can imagine, 'buying the dip' remains one of Wall Street's favorite activities - and rightfully so.

Instead of just telling investors to deploy capital, I prefer the approach of Tiger 21 founder Michael Sonnenfeldt. He brings up a strategy I have also brought up in the past, which is regularly investing during volatile periods and increasing investment as stock prices decline. I added emphasis to the quote below:

A smart move investors can make right now, he said, is to put a systematic strategy in place to increase buying as markets fall, either directly or through ETFs. “Investors can move in stages by investing a set amount when the index drops 20% from its high, double that amount at 25%, and increase further at 30%," said Sonnenfeldt. That “allows investors to take advantage of market volatility without being driven by fear, and to execute a smart offense in a downturn with a strategy that can pay off in the long term.” - Bloomberg

Others are making the case for high-quality income, which is an area I have liked since I started to build my 'growth-to-value' rotation thesis after the pandemic.

In this case, Emily Roland, the co-chief investment strategist at John Hancock Investment Management, makes the case for investment-grade corporate bonds. These are corporate bonds with a credit rating of at least BBB-.

Generally speaking, I don't like the debt market. That's mainly because I like to invest in equity. I want to own a piece of a company for decades. I want to benefit through capital gains and dividend growth, not be a mere provider of debt.

Currently, we're seeing some cracks, as global economic uncertainty has damaged the debt market a bit. As we can see below, the amount of distressed debt (trading below 80% of its par value) has made a comeback.

That's why I wrote, "High-yield debt is a great place for income - until it isn't."

The good news when buying investment-grade corporate debt is that you're lending money to companies like JPMorgan (JPM), Bank of America (BAC), Oracle (ORCL), UnitedHealth (UNH), and others.

These companies are the largest issuers in the iShares iBoxx $ Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (LQD), which currently yields roughly 4.5%. Roughly half of the debt in that ETF has an A-range rating. The other half has a BBB-range rating.

Over the past ten years, LQD has been a much better investment than the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT), which tracks long-term government bonds.

My personal opinion has been for a while that I would rather lend money to an A-rated American corporation with a wide moat than the U.S. government, as I simply prefer the risk/reward.

Moreover, we indeed see a decline in longer-term bond rates, allowing the U.S. Treasury to refinance maturing debt and extend average maturities (as I discussed in this article). I believe corporate bonds are not a bad place to be.

Needless to say, bonds usually don't keep up with stocks. Only buy corporate bonds to add some safety to your portfolio. For younger investors, stocks are usually the best place to be.

Except during recessions. That's when (investment-grade) bonds are the place to be for income and safety.

The next idea is right up my alley, as it's something I spend most of my time on. I'm talking about 'opportunistic plays,' which John Pantekidis, the managing partner of TwinFocus, is focusing on.

What sounds quite fancy is nothing more than a term for stocks that bring something special to the table. Mr. Pantekidis, for example, is buying defense and nuclear stocks, two areas with strong secular growth.

The defense industry is an area I have dedicated a lot of energy to in recent years. In fact, I own five major players in the aerospace and defense industry. I covered all of them in a March 16 article titled "Defending Dividends: Why American Defense Stocks Are The Ultimate Value Play."

I explained why I remain super bullish despite defense spending fears, new disruptors, and global uncertainty. My favorite deep-value play in this area is Lockheed Martin (LMT), which currently yields 3.0%. It generates almost no money from commercial operations and has raised its dividend for more than 20 consecutive years.

Currently trading at just 18.3x earnings, I see a conservative path to >14% annual returns through at least 2027.

Other opportunistic plays with limited risk are midstream companies, the owners of pipelines, storage, and gathering/processing facilities used by oil and gas producers.

Unlike their customers, midstream companies do not care much about the price of oil, gas, and related commodities. To them, it does not matter if oil trades at $60 or $90, as almost all money comes from take-or-pay and long-term contracted operations with consistent inflation escalators.

They benefit from secular growth like rising export demand for North American energy, decades of underinvestment in new pipelines, and the (related) fact that most have shifted their focus from investing in CapEx to growing free cash flow.

However, recent tariff headlines have sent shockwaves through the industry, as even high-quality stocks like ONEOK (OKE), Antero Midstream (AM), Kinder Morgan (KMI), MPLX (MPLX), and others sold off violently. Please note that MPLX is a Master Limited Partnership. All other midstream companies I just mentioned are C-Corps, which do NOT issue K-1 forms.

I believe these sell-offs have two reasons:

  • General panic is causing investors to take profits, while others are forced to cover margin calls. In other words, when markets go south with a ton of volatility, people do not always tend to make the smartest decisions.
  • China announced retaliation. Markets fear that general export demand for key commodities like natural gas liquids will suffer.

While these stock price drawdowns aren't fun, I like them, as I believe fears are overblown. While a recession could temporarily lower demand for energy, the United States will remain a key player in energy. Tariffs won't change that.

For example, the U.S. is the largest exporter of liquefied natural gas, a commodity that most nations simply cannot produce in large quantities.

The same goes for natural gas liquids like propane, which nations like China need a lot of for a wide range of purposes. Over the past ten years, exports of this commodity have grown by more than 4x.

I will be adding to Antero Midstream this week, as I love its 5.5% yield. I am also looking into ONEOK, which I want to buy for my parents. Whereas Antero operates in Appalachia, ONEOK has a bigger footprint in the Permian, where it grows through areas like natural gas liquids and refined products.

If I could, I would also buy MPLX, as I LOVE its business model and its 8% yield. However, because I am European, I cannot invest in MLPs.

Another stock I want to bring up is S&P Global (SPGI), the world's largest rating agency, which has spent decades diversifying its business model. Although the company yields just 0.9%, I wanted to bring it up, as it's one of the companies that's rarely 'cheap.'

One reason is its margin profile, as it has a company-wide operating margin of 49%, one of the highest among service companies. It also benefits from a massive wave of maturing debt. Most of this debt will require a rating to attract a wide pool of buyers.

Over the next 84 months, close to $16 trillion in debt will mature.

Although current tariff fears have muddied the water, a decline in longer-term rates could pave a very favorable way for even higher issuance, potentially making SPGI one of the big winners of the next bull market.

Now turning to the 2024 issuance environment. We saw nearly $4 trillion in billed issuance in 2024, significantly outpacing our expectations, with extremely favorable market conditions, including tight credit spreads and lowering interest rates. We saw many issuers take advantage in 2024 to refinance debt and felt very strong activity in CLO volumes as well as repricing and amend and extend activity. - SPGI 4Q24 Earnings Call

Currently trading at 28x earnings with an annual EPS growth outlook of 10-12%, I believe SPGI is a great buy if markets continue to weaken, as it's a great low-yield 'opportunistic' dividend grower.

Needless to say, as this week progresses and the tariff situation unfolds, we'll discuss a lot more opportunities.

For now, this is my takeaway:

Takeaway

As it turns out, the cash trap isn't a trap, it's a lifeline.

With $7.4 trillion parked in money markets and bearish sentiment at twice the historical average, having dry powder is a blessing for both the market and individual investors.

The good news is that while markets are volatile, history shows that patience wins, as stocks haven't lost money over 20-year periods, and 60/40 portfolios have never been negative after five years.

With that said, for income, investment-grade corporate bonds (like LQD) offer more attractive yields than treasuries. Meanwhile, opportunistic plays like defense stocks, midstream, and even S&P Global, offer attractive risk/rewards.

In general, I like wide-moat dividend stocks, including higher-yielding players and lower-yielding dividend growers. That's why we will continue to discuss new opportunities in the days, weeks, and months ahead.

So, what's the bottom line?

Stay calm, reassess your portfolio, and buy the dip systematically.

And, as always, time in the market beats timing the market, especially when fear is this high.

Risks To My Thesis

To me, the biggest risk is a scenario where inflation remains sticky, preventing the Fed from easing rates and long-term bond yields from falling. This would increase recession and even stagflation risks.

data.title: Morgan Stanley Direct Lending: Why Its Portfolio Is Relatively Insulated Vs. Tariffs
data.description:
Performance Assessment

Morgan Stanley Direct Lending (NYSE:MSDL) has held up better than the S&P 500 (SPY) (SPX) (IVV) (VOO) since my initiating coverage on the stock:

Thesis

After consideration of its Q4 FY24 results and a fast-changing macroeconomic environment, I still find it difficult to make a compelling buy case for MSDL:

  1. Yield and NII margin pressures persist
  2. Tariffs may curb new investment growth
  3. A fall in rates can lower debt costs and drive further deal activity
  4. MSDL is trading at a higher-than-usual premium to peers
  5. Bullish momentum is offset by a key resistance level

Yield and NII margin pressures persist

MSDL's weighted average yield on its investments has been ticking down for the past 4 quarters now:

As of last quarter, this decline in yields has creeped into my metric of annualized yield computed on a net investment income basis as well, in which the positive effects of a growing investments book is counted:

In the Dec'24 earnings call, management attributed these trends to be due to lower base rates and some repricing dynamics with borrowers:

We did see further compression in yields over the last quarter, which were primarily attributable to the decrease in base rates and to a lesser extent repricing dynamics which were more concentrated in the second and third quarter of this year.

- CFO David Pessah in the Q4 FY24 earnings call

But the impact of net investment income [NII] margins is hard to ignore as it has led to a 430bps fall:

...our total investment income was $103 million for the fourth quarter as compared to $110 million in the prior quarter. The decline in our core earnings was driven by the aforementioned impact from the change in portfolio yield and the limited non-recurring income from repayment activity.

- CFO David Pessah in the Q4 FY24 earnings call

This flowed onto a rather underwhelming performance at the PAT level too as results missed expectations:

I think a rebound in these yield and NII metrics is unlikely to occur very soon as I think the BDC faces new pressures from reduced deal flow and falling rates:

Tariffs may curb new investment growth

The talk of the season is tariffs - so how is MSDL affected?

Well, firstly let's take note of management's bold claim that their portfolio has some insulation from tariff impacts:

we are closely monitoring for any potential impacts to our existing portfolio from government reform, including tariffs. We believe that the portfolio should be relatively insulated.

- President Michael Occi in the Q4 FY24 earnings call, Author's bolded highlight

Looking at the numbers describing MSDL's sector exposures in its investments portfolio, I think it is because I only identify 3.6% + 3.8% = 7.4% of exposure from explicitly tariff-sensitive sectors - automobiles and automobile components. The rest of the named sectors are mostly service-oriented and domestic businesses:

But note that this 7.4% is only a minimum exposure. The "Others" section in the portfolio makes up 25.8% and there could be some more tariff-sensitive exposure there. So perhaps overall, we can guesstimate that about 15% of MSDL's portfolio may be impacted particularly by tariffs via higher costs. I would say that's a sizable but not disastrous impact in the relative scheme of things.

But all that discussion is focused on the impact of tariffs on MSDL's existing portfolio. What about its investment portfolio growth prospects?

Here, I have a bit of a more cautious view. This is because it looks like the higher than expected tariffs imposed by the US is leading to an escalated trade war as countries such as China respond back with additional reciprocal tariffs of 34%. And this has spiked US and Global Recession odds from 40% to 60%. In a recessionary environment, it is natural for incremental deal making activity to slow down. Hence, I see some downside risk on the new investment commitment trends going ahead, which have already started to flat-line on a 4-quarter period moving average basis (dotted blue line in the chart below):

Ultimately, I think this can also put some downside pressure on the flat NAV/share figures too:

A fall in rates can lower debt costs and drive further deal activity

I do recognize some bullish risks coming from dovish monetary policy. In my last article on MSDL, one of my viewpoints was that:

MSDL stands to benefit asymmetrically from falling rates

This is because gross asset yields were expected to be more stable compared to debt costs. I still think this may be true.

The good news is that the chances of a rate cut in next month's Fed meeting has recently increased from 10.62% to 33.33%:

I expect this to further drive down the cost of debt further, more sustainably below 7%:

The lower cost of borrowing can also help increase deal activity. Already, MSDL has impressively been able to increase the number of borrowers in its portfolio even in a weaker environment by leveraging Morgan Stanley's business relationships:

Over the course of 2024, more than three quarters of our non-refinancing gross deployment was to new borrowers. While we remain focused on supporting existing borrowers through incremental financing, we think the skew towards new platforms highlights the value of our unique sourcing platform even amidst, in our view, a more subdued LBO environment.

- CEO Jeff Levin in the Q4 FY24 earnings call

So overall, I see rate cuts as sources of bullish risks that may offset some of the tariff-related slowdowns on investment activity.

MSDL is trading at a higher-than-usual premium to peers

I have taken all the companies in the VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD) to form the peers set in the analysis above.

MSDL has typically traded at 1-yr fwd PE discount of 0.9% vs its BDC peers. But now at 8.63x, MSDL stock is at a 5.8% premium to the BDC sector's median of 8.83x. So I would say this reduces the margin of safety for buys.

Bullish momentum is offset by a key resistance level

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

Relative Read of MSDL vs SPX500

The MSDL vs SPX500 charts show strong bullish momentum in the last couple of months. But the ratio prices are also at a key resistance area. Given these counteracting forces, I anticipate a sideways rollover period ahead.

Takeaway & Positioning

MSDL has been weighed down by falling investment yields, which is leading to underwhelming net investment income performance, even after accounting for portfolio growth impacts. And although its current investments portfolio's sector exposures indicate some insulation against the impact of tariffs, I suspect that the second-order effect of a tariff-driven trade war sparking a recession may curb new investment activity growth. That said, a bullish risk I see is that the chances of a rate cut have increased in next month's Fed meeting. This will reduce the cost of debt further for MSDL and also help spur deal activity, which has broadly been resilient thus far even in a weak environment.

On valuations, MSDL is trading at a higher-than-usual premium vs its BDC sector peers. And the relative technicals vs the S&P500 point to a sideways outlook ahead. Hence, I maintain my 'Neutral/Hold' on MSDL.

How to interpret Hunting Alpha's ratings:

Strong Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence. I also have a net long position in the security in my personal portfolio.

Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis

Neutral/hold: Expect the company to perform in-line with the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis

Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis

Strong Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence

The typical time-horizon for my views is multiple quarters to more than a year. It is not set in stone. However, I will share updates on my changes in stance in a pinned comment to this article and may also publish a new article discussing the reasons for the change in view.

data.title: Buy Alert: Trinity Capital Now Offers A 14% Covered Yield
data.description:
Crashing stock prices amid an increase in market volatility have opened up a new buying window for business development company Trinity Capital Inc. (NASDAQ:TRIN).

The stock price of Trinity Capital plunged 7.38% on Friday, causing the business development company’s yield to skyrocket past 14%. That said, though, the BDC covers its dividend with net investment income and has possibly a shot of growing its dividend as well in 2025.

I think that the market crash last week is an excellent opportunity to build positions in a high quality, leading business development company of Trinity Capital’s caliber.

In my last piece on Trinity Capital, I underscored what I perceived to be the core pillars of the underlying investment levers, which were:

  1. Positive interest rate sensitivity;
  2. A robust dividend; and
  3. The offering of specialized financing solutions, particularly in the equipment market, which separated the BDC from other business development companies.

Moreover, Trinity Capital’s positive trend in total investment income as well as a much more affordable valuation on the dip are two more reasons why Trinity Capital remains my favorite BDC to buy.

Growing Portfolio Value, Total Investment Income And Yields

Trinity Capital is primarily focused on providing term loans to companies that have raised equity and already have demonstrated that they have some kind of institutional support.

Term loans accounted for the vast majority of the business development company’s investments in 4Q24; 75% of all investments were term loans, whereas a minority of 18.3% went to so called equipment-financing deals. These deals typically involve some kind of loan made to companies that need to invest in fixed assets or working capital as they scale their business up.

Though the 14% dividend yield that Trinity Capital sells suggests that there might be some coverage or performance issues with the business development, this is not the case. Trinity Capital has a very positive trend in total investment income, which relates back predominantly to new originations the BDC has been pursuing.

Trinity Capital’s total investment income in 4Q24 amounted to $70.8 million, up 48% YoY. In the last four quarters, which was a time during which Trinity Capital had a filled investment pipeline that led to a surge in new term loans particularly, the portfolio value skyrocketed 35% YoY to $1.73 billion.

In my view, the trend in total investment income (the blue bar chart in the below graphic) suggests that Trinity Capital is going to profit from an increasing margin of safety in 2025.

Portfolio Yields Probably To Remain High In 2025

Trinity Capital’s portfolio yields, as of the end of 4Q24, were 16.4% and rose three consecutive quarters amid a strong lending environment and a big opportunity set for new originations.

Moreover, the central bank has taken its foot off the gas in terms of interest rate reductions, which supports Trinity Capital’s lending and origination activity in 2025.

Pay-Out Performance: Trinity Capital Vs. BDC Peers

Trinity Capital had record annual net investment income in 2024 and, thanks to growing total investment income, the business development company profited from a decrease in its pay-out ratio. While Trinity Capital’s dividend pay-out ratio was 94% or higher every single quarter between 1Q24 and 3Q24, the term loan boost and associate income increase have caused an improvement in the BDC’s pay-out health.

The dividend pay-out ratio fell 6.5% QoQ to 88% in 4Q24. If this trend of growing total investment income continues in 2025, which I anticipate, Trinity Capital should be able to push its dividend pay-out ratio even lower, and quite possibly below the peer group average.

In addition, with improving dividend health, I think that the business development company could potentially even hike its dividend in 2025.

We can compare Trinity Capital to other business development companies in the sector in terms of dividend pay-out ratio. Compared to peers, Trinity Capital still has a relatively high present level of NII, the dividend pay-out ratio could fall below the peer group average of 88% in 2025.

Why Passive Income Investors Should Buy The Dip

The tariff tantrum last week has opened up a new buying window, particularly for high-yielding business development companies, many of which cover their dividends with net investment income, including Trinity Capital.

At the time of writing, Trinity Capital is selling for a 9% premium to net asset value which as of December 31, 2024 stood at $13.35 per share, up 1.2% YoY.

However, this does not make Trinity Capital the most expensive BDC in the peer group. Sixth Street Specialty Lending, Inc. (TSLX) is selling for a 19% premium to NAV and is presently the most expensive BDC investment in the peer group.

After last week’s market rout, other business development companies are in the bargain bin also, including Blackstone Secured Lending Fund. (BXSL) which is selling for 1.10x net asset value. Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC) has completely lost its premium valuation (and now sells for 1.02x net asset value), and might very well start selling for less than NAV in the coming days.

For Trinity Capital, I think a 1.15x Price-To-NAV is an achievable goal (reflecting an implied intrinsic value of $15.35 per share) as long as the business development company does not lose traction with its total investment income growth.

Moreover, as I explained, I think that the trend in total investment income points to lower dividend pay-out metrics, which obviously equates to a higher margin of safety.

Times like these should be used by passive income investors, in my view, to aggressively build long positions in leading business development companies that have proven over time that they can maintain high pay-outs or even grow them.

BDCs that I am presently buying are Trinity Capital, Blackstone Secured Lending Fund and, more than any other, Ares Capital. I will add to my Ares Capital position particularly aggressively in the event that the stock falls below net asset value and a discount valuation becomes available.

Why The Investment Thesis Might Be More Bearish

The market nose-dived last week over fears that the aggressive stand that the Trump Administration has taken in terms of tariffs could potentially backfire and put the U.S. economy into a recession. A recession, in turn would increase pressure on the central bank to lower short-term interest rates, which would be a negative for BDCs that have positive interest rate sensitivity (and floating-rate loan exposure).

I think that the improving dividend pay-out ratio counterbalances this risk, but falling interest rates are clearly a possible headwind for Trinity Capital in 2025.

My Conclusion

Trinity Capital is a very attractive and appealing investment option for passive income investors as the market panics and really good investment bargains become available. Trinity Capital is seeing a positive trend in its total investment income, catalyzed by portfolio and specifically term loan growth.

This in turn has led to an improvement in the BDC’s dividend pay-out ratio, creating a possible path for Trinity Capital to raise its dividend in 2025.

As a consequence, the 14% yield that Trinity Capital’s stock sells for in the market after last week’s tariff tantrum is much safer than investors think it is. Buy.

data.title: Recession-Proofing Your Portfolio
data.description:
On Wednesday, Trump's "Liberation Day" ended decades of the free-trade era responsible for global wealth creation. On Thursday, we woke up to a new reality: stock averages down 10% in two days—the largest wealth wipe-out since the COVID-19-induced crash.

The announced universal duties of at least 10% on all imports, with some going much higher for China (54%), the European Union (30%), and Japan (24%), will be responsible for the self-induced recession, reduction in purchasing power and higher inflation.

However, this is not only about the higher cost of goods. Take, for instance, the commentary from Bob Iger, the CEO of Walt Disney (DIS), who said that it'd be challenging for US companies to shift production from overseas back to the US due to different workforce expertise and skills. If you recall, the delays in the opening of new chip fabs in the US by Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM), one of the main reasons cited was a lack of chip-production skilled labor. This will create even more significant issues for companies like Apple (AAPL) that rely on the unique skillset of Chinese workers in Foxconn's (OTCPK:FXCOF) production facilities.

Some market participants still believe Trump's announced tariffs on its allies and foes is purely a negotiating tactic, to secure better deals, but once you let the Genie out of the bottle, it can't be reversed, and we are in a tit-for-tat trade war with China announcing 34% levies on all US imports, with EU preparing their own package, most likely aimed at digital services, crown jewels of US economy.

As we can see from the magnitude of the stock market movement over a span of two days, the market was clearly not ready and did not price the tariff risk. Negotiations still may bring the tariffs down, not eliminate them, yet the global economy is poised for a rough ride.

The US economy hasn't been in great shape prior to the announcement, with analysts expecting growth of 0 to -1% in Q1 2025, without considering the impact of duties. Most duties will be effective as of 9th April, an incremental hit for the US economy in Q2, likely entering recession in Q2 or Q3 2025.

I think in Q2, as a result of the levies, US economy is likely to contract 2-3%, not only impacted directly by the higher cost of goods that'll have a negative effect on consumer spending that makes up 70% of US GDP, but disrupt global supply chains, with potential product shortages, pushing prices even higher. Let's not forget the retaliatory action by trading partners that'll pressure US product competitiveness abroad and profit margins.

How many of the tariffs will be over the mid-term negotiated away remains to be seen, but I don't expect them to go away. The combination of lofty valuations we saw early in 2025, combined with contracting growth, reminds me of early 2001, when the US entered relatively mild recessions lasting from March 2001 to November 2001, yet the S&P 500 (SP500) fell 45% peak-to-through and Nasdaq (QQQ) dropped nearly 80% as an excessive investment in internet-related companies inflated stock prices.

At that time, the GDP contracted only slightly and unemployment rose from 4% to 6% by mid-2003, with the Fed cutting rates from 6.5% in 2000 down to 1.75% by the end of 2001. Now, I don't expect such a fierce stock plunge as tech valuations were not as fragile as before, supported by strong profitability and lower P/E, but we could easily see 20-30% pullback in S&P 500 this year to $4,500 at a mid-point, a level I am willing to start increasing my currently reduced equity exposure, but I am waiting first on EU's retaliation.

Speaking of concrete economics numbers or the S&P 500's EPS contraction at this stage appears a bit premature because this is more about just the economy—it's about politics.

However, what we can talk about, and what I aim to make a centerpiece of this article, is what sectors appear to be proven for significant hit (ones I avoid) and what sectors, on the other hand, appear to be ripped to deliver alpha.

Where I See Risks

Automotive, Construction & Heavy Equipment, Machinery, and Electronics are all businesses that are in this new trade war most exposed due to RM tariffs, in particular steel, aluminum, rare earths, chips, and other critical inputs.

The carmakers such as Tesla (TSLA), Ford (F), and General Motors (GM), in particular, spent decades building their lean supply chains, relying on sourcing steel and car components from around the world, will struggle to pass on the increased costs, likely having to absorb some of the costs, leading to margin contraction in already tight margin business.

Moving production back to the US? Yes, inevitably, that may happen, but not earlier than 3–5 years, and with cars at much higher costs.

Next are Construction & Heavy Equipment makers, such as Caterpillar (CAT) or Deere & Company (DE), that rely on steel, cement, aluminum, copper, and other industrial metals will be impacted.

But that's not all. If we now speak outside of any potential retaliatory action by trading partners, the big tech will feel the heat of domestic duties alone. 90% of the world's chips are produced in Taiwan. The 32% tariff on the imports will make all the cutting-edge chips designed by NVIDIA (NVDA), used in data centers, more expensive, likely passing the costs to their customers such as Amazon (AMZN), Meta (META) and Microsoft (MSFT).

Apple is perhaps among the hardest-hit big tech businesses. The 54% levy on China-produced iPhones is estimated to push the price of the current top model iPhone 16 Pro Max from $1,599 to $2,300, if Apple is to pass the cost onto customers. If not, Apple will have to absorb a portion of the cost, shrinking its profit margins.

That's not all.

Consumer discretionary businesses, such as Best Buy (BBY) and Target (TGT), all warned about price increases that'll have a negative impact on consumer spending, with already stretched consumers.

In the past, defense manufacturer Lockheed Martin (LMT) and partial defense/civil aerospace manufacturer Boeing (BA) might have been as safe havens as defense spending is not cyclical, however in particular, here I see major risk not because of tariffs but current political narrative. Historically, fighter jets such as the advanced F-35 and F-16 Fighting Falcon were among key exports to allies in Europe, but Trump's rhetoric about pulling out of NATO or threatening to capture Greenland raises concerns, with ally countries less likely to buy US-made weapons in the future due to loss of trust. Just imagine that the US will threaten Denmark with weapon deactivation if they don't let Greenland go. Yes, replacing US-made fighter jets, with alternatives won't be an easy feat, but fewer orders or cancellations will pressure the top and bottom lines of these companies.

Where I Think Money Is "Safe-er"?

I flagged basically in most of my articles since the beginning of this year that I was heading into 2025 with a record cash pile due to lofty valuations and trimming more holdings in the process due to the heightened uncertainty, particularly as Trump announced the go-ahead with tariffs on Canada and Mexico in February.

Now, there are certain companies that I hold in my portfolio, despite not being recession-proof, but with a long-term investing mindset, trimming the positions due to increased volatility would be a mistake. Instead, I aim to add more to these positions, but likely there will be more pain, which, in return, allows a dollar-cost average.

For instance, Dutch ASML (ASML) pulled back meaningfully, opening the opportunity to add more shares, as the company remains one of the critical points in the whole semiconductor industry, with its cutting-edge EUV lithography machines, even if growth slows.

Other great businesses that I own in my portfolio, such as LVMH (OTCPK:LVMHF), Visa (V), Alphabet (GOOGL) and MSCI (MSCI) all seen pain as well. But am I selling? Certainly, I don't, even if I expect more weakness; instead, I am preparing to buy more shares because, in the long term, these companies will likely be winners.

From the strength perspective, I expect healthcare businesses with a lesser production footprint in the EU to do well. Communication services and domestic utility companies are not exposed directly to tariffs, yet the cost of their services may rise indirectly.

For instance, Walmart (WMT) and Costco (COST) are relatively well positioned, as consumer staples, particularly as these companies buy in-bulk, enabling to negotiate better discounts and likely gain market share. Yet, I don't recommend buying them right now, as Walmart's P/E ratio of 33x and Costco's P/E at 53x are far too high, at EPS growth of around 8-12%.

What I recommend buying instead, is fixed-income (SHY), in particular US Treasury Bills, as FED's funds rates are still in restrictive territory and likely will stay in the face of higher inflation. Even if rates were to rise (not my base case), T-Bills with 12M expiry face limited maturity risk.

With a 3.86% yield, it's a no-brainer trade in the face of the heightened uncertainty.

As a younger, more aggressive investor, relatively often I prefer to have cash ready to deploy during market weakness, as generally buying high-quality businesses has proven to be the right play if one has 5-10 years investment horizon, but today's situation is different, and we could see a prolonged period of weakness, where protecting one's funds against inflation is of utmost importance.

Warren Buffett, with his Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) follows a similar strategy, being a net seller of equities for nine consecutive quarters and instead pilling the record-cash pile into short-duration treasuries. BRK's cash & short-term investments stood at $334B by the end of 2024, ready to strike if the opportunity comes.

Now, I don't think Berkshire has yet deployed any of this record capital in 2025, and I would expect Buffet to wait till the dust settles first.

I owned Berkshire in my portfolio for years, following Buffet's preaching, and while today's valuation is not most favorable, confirmed by the lack of any buybacks, I was adding shares as I see the company as a safe haven with 42% of its portfolio worth in cash, getting the best capital-allocation in class.

The last asset class I want to cover today is gold.

To make it clear, I am not an enthusiast of commodities due to the lack of liquidity, high transactions/storage fees, and the lack of cash flow.

However, seeing uncertainty at such steep levels as we do today, changed my perspective and gold, currently trading at $2,978/ounce, is a testament that investors still see this precious metal as the go-to safe heaven.

If the uncertainty continues, I expect we could see the price at $3,100-3,300/ounce by the year-end.

Commodities are historically prone to significant fluctuations, but what makes the case for gold more attractive is the weakening USD. Gold is priced in USD globally, so when the dollar weakens, as we are seeing right now, it costs more dollars to buy the same amount of gold, pushing the price up.

Gold proved its defensive character during Black Monday in 1987, the Dot-com Bubble in 2001, the GFC in 2008, and also the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020, and I expect it will keep performing well relative to the stock market in the 2025 recession.

Investor's Takeaway

In a nutshell, if tariffs are here to stay at the same level as announced, the US economy and global economy are heading for a recession.

At this stage, it's not only about economic implications but politics as well. We have seen China retaliate with 34% tariffs on all US imported goods, saving its face in front of its domestic audience, irrespective of the economic damage.

Even though I expect some of the tariffs will be re-negotiated, once you let the Genie out of the bottle, there is no way back.

I see industrials, consumer discretionary, electronics, and defense contractors as the most exposed in this economic/political battle, but few pockets of opportunity exist to park cash during this uncertain period.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Asset Bubble Bug Finds Another Windshield
data.description:
Financial commentators predictably blame the Trump administration for 'unexpected' market losses.

Two things about that: the tariff file was not unexpected. Trump has promised to do this for a long time. Secondly, major downdraft cycles are the natural and recurring aftermath of debt abuse and asset bubbles.

Reckless risk-taking was an international pastime during the years of ‘easy’ money monetary and fiscal policies; it seemed fun while it lasted, but it was always a bug waiting for a windshield — bubbles burst every time — it’s what they do. The progress to date in some widely held benchmarks is summarized below.

The damage has been relatively light so far. As I have repeatedly pointed out, the average stock market decline during past recessions has been 30%, and 34% to 86% when valuations begin from extreme highs, as in 1929, 1973, 1999, 2007, 2019, 2022 and 2024. If prices are halfway through this bear market decline, historically, that would be a fairly mild loss cycle. These are the ladders and snakes of financial mania: Years of apparent progress are deleted in weeks and months. Most are taken by surprise.

The masses have no business gambling with savings they cannot afford or stomach to lose, especially for those at or within a few years of retirement. How long will it take for assets to recover losses this time?

It’s not too late to review risk exposures. It’s also worth repeating that real estate downturns have historically led to the harshest recessions, and that’s happening now.

I’ve diligently measured and mapped this incoming mess; frontline reports now confirm the unwinding. Insolvency trustees have entered their boom time. Hoyes, Mikalos’ consumer solvency counsellor Scott Terrio wrote this on LinkedIn last week:

Many people entered the housing market due to FOMO; many of them had really no business doing so as they couldn’t afford the homes they bought; yet buy them they did – somehow – co-signors, or with many qualifications being very suspect (mortgage-to-income ratios that would make your eyes water). ANYTHING to not miss out. But that kind of thinking can end up backfiring.

In many cases, things would’ve been ok had everything gone ok. But life isn’t like that, very often. The tipping points from doing ok into insolvency (whether legal insolvency or just technical insolvency) are often fine margins. Some of those factors are done by choice, even if never really intentionally.

In matters of personal finance, avoidance is a huge protection, but it takes proactive decision-making and good advice. And there seems to be less and less good advice either on the internet or in terms of peer pressure. Avoidability.

No truer words were ever spoken; none of this was unforeseeable. There was always going to be a catalyst for the delirium to break.

We have another opportunity to review financially destructive behaviours and make wiser choices that can improve health and stability for the rest of our lives. But we have to admit, repent and reform to recover. Repeating the same patterns and expecting different outcomes is just plain painful.

Disclosure: No positions

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Dividend Cut Alert: Blue Chips With 9%+ Yields Getting Too Risky
data.description:
Investors turn to blue-chip high-yield dividend stocks because they want to be able to enjoy attractive and ideally growing income over the long term through thick and thin. However, the recent tariff announcements by the Trump administration have called into question the safety of quite a few blue-chip dividend stocks, given the severity of them and the potential for it to lead to a severe recession. On top of that, the growing odds of recession have also greatly boosted the market's expectations of Federal Reserve rate cuts, with Mr. Market now pricing in about five rate cuts this year. This, in turn, will have consequences on businesses that have exposure to short-term interest rates. In this article, I am going to discuss two blue-chip stocks and share why I think that their dividends are increasingly at risk of being cut.

Risky Dividend #1

The first stock I am going to discuss is a blue-chip BDC (BIZD) called Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL). It has one of the highest quality underlying portfolios with 98% of its investments in first-lien senior secured debt, very low non-accruals at just 0.3% on an amortized cost basis, and an investment-grade balance sheet. It is backed by the Blackstone (BX) credit team, which is one of the most impressive players in the credit space in the world, as BX itself is the largest alternative asset manager in the world.

It has also posted strong results since going public by growing its net asset value per share, its dividend per share, posting strong returns on equity, low non-accruals, and overall general total return outperformance. However, I have warned recently that its stock price had gotten ahead of itself, and that it was now overvalued. This warning proved prescient as, over the past week, it has plummeted by over 14%, significantly underperforming better-priced peers that I was more positive on by a considerable amount.

Now that its price-to-NAV multiple is back at a more reasonable level, I am still not overly bullish on the stock, as I believe its dividend is now facing a more risky outlook over the next year. In Q4, it generated $0.84 per share in net investment income while paying out a $0.77 per share base dividend. While that 109% coverage ratio looks pretty reasonable for a BDC, especially given that BDCs are expected to pay out 90% of their taxable income in order to maintain their BDC status, the macro clouds forming on the horizon indicate that BXSL's base dividend may not be sustainable. This is because, in addition to the potential for a rise in non-accruals due to growing recession concerns from the Trump tariffs, the market is now also pricing in a high likelihood of interest rates falling by 100 basis points by the end of the year.

As the below snippet from its 2024 10-K illustrates, a 100 basis point decline in interest rates would provide a roughly $100 million hit to its net investment income, given that BXSL generated $183 million in Q4 NII, which annualizes to $732 million.

That indicates a 13.7% reduction in net investment income by the end of the year simply due to interest rates declining. This does not take into account potential additional headwinds from a rise in non-accruals, as well as a potential loss in fee income from a potential decline in deal-making activity due to the tariffs causing a rise in uncertainty.

On top of that, BXSL has very low fixed interest rate debt maturing beginning in January 2026, which it will likely have to refinance at higher rates, especially if interest rates only decline by 100 basis points between now and the end of the year. This would likely provide another headwind, further reducing their net investment income.

When all that is put together, it appears that BXSL's current base dividend is unlikely to be sustainable if the Fed indeed cuts four times this year, especially if there is even a modest uptick in non-accruals due to economic weakness. As a result, while I think BXSL is a fine BDC as BDCs go, its base dividend coverage ratio is a little bit too tight for comfort, and if current macro headwinds on the horizon persist, it will likely have to contend with a base dividend reduction sometime over the next year.

Risky Dividend #2

Another blue-chip dividend stock whose dividend is likely going to come under increasing pressure moving forward is LyondellBasell Industries (LYB). LYB has a strong dividend growth track record, as it has increased its payout every year since it started paying one in 2011. It boasts a solid mid-single-digit dividend CAGR in recent years that has kept up with the high inflation post-COVID quite nicely.

On top of that, on a normalized basis, it covers its dividend quite easily, as even in 2024, as the business began to face some challenges, its payout ratio was quite conservative at just 57%, and it typically has a very high cash conversion ratio at between 83% and 98% cash conversion levels since 2019. On top of that, it has a fortress balance sheet with an investment-grade credit rating and $3.4 billion of cash and cash equivalents on hand as of the end of 2024, with a fairly modest 1.8 times net debt to adjusted EBITDA.

That being said, the chemical sector is getting hit hard right now, and while LYB has managed to outperform peers like Dow Inc. (DOW) by a considerable margin, it is still down by over 40% over the last year alone.

This is because there is weak demand and high-cost pressures on the business that are pinching its profits. As a result, it suffered meaningful downgrades recently, including UBS slashing its 2025 and 2026 EBITDA forecast by over 10% for each year. As a result, it will likely generate much less free cash flow in the coming years, especially if it sustains its current more aggressive CAPEX profile as it tries to enter the advanced plastics recycling and sustainability business. And of course, it has a huge cash pile, which should be able to support its dividend even if it does not fully cover its dividend in the near term. But at some point, the company is going to have to make a decision about what to do about its dividend if conditions do not improve, especially if they continue to deteriorate based on current headwinds from tariffs, energy price movements, and general oversupply in the marketplace.

This is particularly troublesome because the US tends to export a considerable amount of polyethylene to China, and this could be disrupted due to the ongoing trade war between the two countries. As a result, while LYB has been executing well, has a very strong balance sheet at the moment, and based on a backward look at its results, has a very well-covered dividend that management even very recently reiterated its intent to continue growing, the growing macro storm may simply be too much and force the company to abandon its dividend growth ambitions and potentially even cut its dividend.

Therefore, while I still like the company as part of a diversified portfolio due to its strong underlying fundamentals and recent execution, as well as its long-term growth potential and a very attractive yield based on a normalized earnings environment, investors should be very careful and manage their exposure accordingly. I think this is very important, as current macro headwinds could force management to slash the dividend, which could result in significant further downside for the stock.

Of course, management can also cut back on growth capex spending if it wants to in order to sustain the balance sheet and dividend, and there is also a decent chance that current headwinds could be lifted sometime in the coming months or years. On the other hand, there is no guarantee that management, despite LYB's strong dividend growth track record and management's recent comments in support of continuing to pay and grow the dividend, will continue to prioritize the dividend moving forward if headwinds persist.

Investor Takeaway

As this article points out, there are massive headwinds facing the global economy right now due to the uncertainty caused and potential major reduction in business and economic activity and investment due to the huge tariffs being put in place by the Trump administration. While we do think this is creating some exciting buying opportunities due to broad-based selling in the markets, as we recently discussed here, there are other blue-chip dividend stocks whose previously thought-to-be-safe payouts are now being challenged. Maintaining proper portfolio diversification and guarding against such dividend cuts is a priority of ours in our portfolio at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Mach Natural Resources: Solid Natural Gas Prices Help Its Near-Term FCF
data.description:
Over half (approximately 54%) of Mach Natural Resources' (NYSE:MNR) production is natural gas, compared to around 23% oil. Natural gas strip prices have held up better than oil strip prices recently, so Mach is still projected to generate around $2.31 per unit in 2025 free cash flow at current strip.

Mach's legacy asset production has been declining a bit, though, so I can see its 2026 free cash flow ending up a bit lower. At current 2026 strip (including $60 WTI oil), Mach's 2026 free cash flow may be around $1.75 per unit, while at $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas, that may increase to around $2 per unit.

I've lowered my long-term WTI oil price expectation to $70, which results in Mach's estimated value being trimmed to $15 to $17 per unit. This is a $1.75 per unit reduction compared to its estimated value at $75 WTI oil. Mach now looks slightly undervalued for a longer-term $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas scenario.

Updated 2025 Outlook

Mach did not change its production guidance for 2025 despite its Flycatcher acquisition adding a modest amount (estimated at +1% to +2%) to its 2025 production.

I had previously modeled Mach's 2025 production including the additional volumes from Flycatcher, but have decided to model it based on its original guidance now.

The Flycatcher acquisition probably gives Mach a bit of room to trim capex later in 2025 in response to mediocre oil prices while still meeting its full-year guidance.

The current strip for 2025 involves slightly under $64 WTI oil and $4 NYMEX natural gas, and at those commodity prices I project Mach to generate $936 million in revenues after hedges.

Mach has around 42% of its 2025 oil production hedged at an average of $70.75 and approximately 42% of its 2025 natural gas production hedged at an average of $3.67.

Type Barrels/Mcf $ Per Barrel/Mcf $ Million
Oil 6,752,500 $62.75 $424
NGLs 6,752,500 $21.25 $143
Gas 95,812,500 $3.60 $345
Midstream Operating Profit $17
Hedge Value $7
Total Revenues $936

I have assumed that Mach's 2025 capex budget comes in at the low-end of its guidance range (with the potential budget trimming that I mentioned above).

This would result in Mach generating $273 million ($2.31 per unit) in 2025 free cash flow at current strip prices.

$ Million
Lease Operating $188
Gathering and Processing $93
Taxes Other Than Income $50
Cash G&A $32
Cash Interest $40
Capex $260
Total $663

Debt And 2025 Distributions

Mach reported having $510 million outstanding under its new credit facility as on March 12, 2025. However, Mach's $0.50 per unit quarterly distribution for Q4 2024 was due to be paid out on March 13, 2025. That distribution payment should have been approximately $59 million.

Mach likely has some cash on hand though (it had $106 million in cash on hand at the end of 2024), so it may have been able to make that distribution without further credit facility borrowings.

Mach's new credit facility has $750 million in commitments and matures in February 2029 now.

Pro forma for various transactions, Mach may have started 2025 with approximately $466 million in net debt.

If Mach pays out $2 per unit in 2025 distributions, it would end 2025 with $430 million in net debt (not including the effect of any working capital changes). This would be leverage of 0.75x, which is reasonable to me.

Future Free Cash Flow

Largely due to declining production, Mach's free cash flow (and thus its distribution) seems reasonably likely to be lower in 2026 than the $2.31 per unit that I am projected for 2025.

At current strip (roughly $60 WTI oil and $4.25 Henry Hub natural gas) for 2026, Mach may end up with around $1.75 per unit in free cash flow. At my long-term commodity prices of $70 WTI oil and $3.75 Henry Hub natural gas instead, this would improve to around $2.00 per unit in free cash flow. This assumes a capex budget of around $250 million, with 2026 production having a mid-to-high single digits decline compared to 2025.

Mach may make more acquisitions to help offset the production declines from its legacy assets.

Notes On Valuation

I've reduced Mach's estimated value to $15 to $17 per unit, which is a reduction of $1.75 per unit compared to before. This is mostly due to the effect of changing my long-term WTI oil price estimate from $75 to $70. I have kept my long-term natural gas price estimate at $3.75.

I've reduced my estimate of Mach's value by around 10% compared to a few months ago, while its unit price is down closer to 20%. Thus, I am now modestly positive on Mach at its current price.

Conclusion

Mach's production is primarily natural gas, so a 2025 scenario with mid-$60s oil and roughly $4 natural gas still results in it being projected to generate around $2.31 per unit in free cash flow. This should help support a distribution of around $2 per unit for the year, allowing for a bit of debt reduction.

Mach has typically been spending less than maintenance capex, though, so its legacy production is expected to decline into 2026. This will likely result in lower free cash flow in 2026, although Mach may make further acquisitions to offset the legacy production declines.

I now estimate Mach's value at $15 to $17 per unit, based on my updated long-term commodity prices of $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Medicare Bump Lifts Insurers
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Health insurers rally after CMS increases payment to Medicare Advantage plans. (00:21) Nvidia (NVDA) closes deal to acquire startup Lepton AI: report. (01:41) China pledges to 'fight to the end' against new U.S. tariffs, escalating the trade dispute. (02:38)

This is an abridged transcript.

Shares of leading health insurers rose in post-market trading Monday after the U.S. Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) announced it has substantially raised government payments to Medicare Advantage compared to an initial proposal.

In January, the CMS issued its Advance Notice for the next calendar year, proposing to raise government payments to Medicare Advantage plans by an average of 4.33%, an increase of over $21B compared to 2025.

Per the agency's final rate decision on Monday, payments to Medicare Advantage companies will rise by 5.06% on average from 2025 to 2026, translating to an increase of 2.83 percentage points since the Advance Notice or over $25B increase in MA payments.

The move follows a recent decision where Medicare coverage for expensive obesity medications was declined.

Leading health insurers are rallying in reaction to the announcement. Humana (NYSE:HUM) is up about 10%, Elevance Health (NYSE:ELV) is up 6%, UnitedHealth (NYSE:UNH), the largest insurer in the Medicare Advantage market is up 5.6%, and Clover Health (NASDAQ:CLOV) is up 3.7%.

Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) has closed its acquisition to buy artificial intelligence startup Lepton AI, The Information reported.

The deal to acquire Lepton AI, which resells Nvidia's graphics processing units in a cloud service, was worth “hundreds of millions of dollars,” the news outlet added. Lepton AI's co-founders, Yangqing Jia and Junjie Bai, will stay on at Nvidia now that the deal has closed, the news outlet added, citing people familiar with the matter.

Talk of an acquisition popped up late last month.

Nvidia declined to comment when contacted by Seeking Alpha.

Lepton competes with companies like Together AI in the business of renting out Nvidia GPUs. Founded two years ago, Lepton AI secured an $11M seed round in May 2023 from CRV and Fusion Fund. Meanwhile, Together AI, a key competitor in the server rental space, has raised over $500M in venture capital, despite being only about a year older than Lepton.

President Trump's renewed tariff threats on Monday have prompted China to vow a 'fight to the end,'.

China sharply criticized the U.S.'s escalating tariff threats as "blackmail".

Beijing's commerce ministry stated it would "resolutely take countermeasures to safeguard its rights and interests" if the U.S. proceeds with additional tariffs, calling Trump's actions "a mistake on top of a mistake." The ministry further declared that China would "never accept this" and would "fight to the end.”

This strong condemnation came after Trump threatened a new 50% tariff on Chinese goods effective Wednesday, on top of the current 54%, unless Beijing retracted its retaliatory tariffs – a 34% levy on all U.S. imports starting Thursday, announced in response to a prior U.S. tariff hike.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

Vietnam will buy defense goods from U.S. to reduce trade gap, seeks 45-day tariff delay

Trump says EU must buy $350B of US energy to get tariff relief, rejects ‘zero-for-zero’ proposal

BlackRock’s Fink sees ‘buying opportunity’ during market selloff

Catalyst watch:

  • The two-day Stifel Virtual Targeted Oncology Forum will include participation from Compugen (CGEN), Zymeworks (ZYME), Innate Pharma (IPHA), and Revolution Medicines (RVMD).

  • Atlassian (TEAM) will hold its three-day Team25 event in Anaheim, California. CEO Mike Cannon-Brookes will be one of the keynote speakers.

  • Paris Blockchain Week will begin. Featured speakers include Yves Choueifaty of TOBAM, Anthony Scaramucci of Skybridge, and Jean-Marie Mognetti of CoinShares.

Now let’s take a look at the markets ahead of the opening bell. Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the green. Crude oil is up 0.2% at $60/barrel. Bitcoin is flat at $79,000. Gold is up 0.8% at $3,005.

In the world markets, the FTSE 100 is up 1.7% and the DAX is up 1.6%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Levi Strauss (NYSE:LEVI) +10% – The stock surged after the company delivered mixed Q1 results, surpassing profit expectations.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 11:00 am Treasury Buyback Announcement

  • 1:00 pm 3-Yr Note Auction

data.title: YETI: Beloved Brand With Too Many Risks At The Moment
data.description:
To say it’s been a volatile week for the markets would be a massive understatement. Trump’s new tariffs have severely shaken global markets as the S&P 500 was down over 5% Friday and is down nearly 14% YTD. It’s been even worse from the Nasdaq, which is now in bear market territory as it’s down over 17% YTD.

Many retailers and consumer discretionary stocks, like Nike (NKE) and Lululemon (LULU) have been especially hammered. YETI Holdings, Inc. (NYSE:YETI) hasn’t been exempt from the market bloodbath, as YETI’s stock is down more than 7% this week and is down over 20% YTD.

In the fall, I stated YETI was a buy in part because of the company’s international growth. I am still a believer in the business, yet I’m not looking to rush to add shares given investors are now in a much more uncertain environment.

Let’s dig into YETI's recent financial results and discuss how Trump's tariffs could impact the business in the months (and perhaps) years to come.

Impact of Trump’s Tariffs and Future Risks

First off, let’s discuss the countries that were hit hardest by Trump’s tariffs. As Trump mentioned (or illustrated by that now infamous large tariff board) the United States is placing a 34% tariff rate on China, 49% on Cambodia, 46% on Vietnam, 36% on Thailand, 32% on Taiwan, and 24% on Japan.

As I noted in my prior article, YETI was already working to move production outside of China. Management stated on the prior earnings call that at the end of fiscal year 2024, 20% of global drinkware production would take place outside of China and the goal was to get that number up to 50% by the end of 2025.

As YETI noted in their 10K filing, in 2024, for coolers and equipment products 36% are produced in two large manufacturing sites and for drinkware products 74% are produced at those two sites. The 10K doesn’t go into specifics on the locations of those sites but given the commentary from management I’d assume one or both sites are located in China. As you can see below graphic from a recent investor presentation, all of the drinkware manufacturing sites are in eastern Asia:

On the last earnings call, there were very limited discussions surrounding tariffs, as it was obviously a fluid situation. On their Q1 2025 earnings call, it will be interesting to hear how much progress was made in diverting production to other sites such as Mexico or Italy. Clearly, YETI’s management has been making plans around these tariffs, particularly those impacting China. Yet, it’s clearly a significant material risk which the company also noted in their 10K, stating Trump’s tariffs could cause margin erosion and loss of customers if YETI is forced to pass the price increase to consumers.

Another negative ramification I could see to the business is the delay of product launches. As management noted during the last earnings call, the company is expecting more growth in the second half of the year, and one of the factors of that growth is product launches. Here’s what YETI’s CFO Mike McMullen had to say about why the business is forecasting more growth in the back half of 2025, “...We think it'll be, essentially flat – or drinkware in total will be flat in the first half, down in Q1. But as we look to the second half, I think there's a couple of things to call out. One, we do have the pacing of our innovation is much more second half weighted, this year. And I think one of the drivers of that is, all the work we're doing around our supply chain and diversification of that, I mean, that is, it does have an impact.”

As you can see this supply chain work does impact product launches and depending on the amount of progress that was made in Q1 this could determine if YETI can launch their products in the back half of 2025 on time or if some will have to pushed back into 2026 or beyond as management focuses on diverting production to limit the impact of the tariffs.

Financials

I think Trump’s tariffs will impact the cost of goods sold far more than it will impact revenue, as the majority of YETI’s revenues are generated domestically. As you can see from the table below, although international revenue has been growing, it still only accounted for roughly 19% of total revenue:

Also, YETI’s biggest international presence is currently in Canada and Australia. Two countries which weren’t as impacted by Trump’s tariffs as many others. On the earnings call, management called out expanding into Europe (specifically the U.K.) as well as growing awareness of the brand in Japan. Japan was hit with a higher tariff as I noted above, but generally these are all more “friendly” countries compared to say China or Russia, so hopefully country leaders could negotiate and reach reasonable terms.

One positive for YETI is that the company has diverse products from different revenue channels. As you can see below, nearly a decade ago basically all of YETI’s sales came from their Wholesale channel, now DTC accounts for 60%:

Also, both channels grew compared to fiscal year 2023. For the full year, DTC revenues came in at roughly $1,087 million, which is a 9% increase compared to the prior year. Wholesale revenue came in at $742 million, which is a 12% compared to 2023.

Similarly, from a category perspective, both Drinkwear and Coolers and Equipment grew compared to 2023. In 2024, Drinkware generated $1,094 million in revenue, which was a 7% increase compared to the prior year. Coolers and Equipment generated revenue of $698 million, which is a 17% increase compared to 2023.

If we zoom out and take a look at the big picture, YETI has done a great job of growing revenue and gross profit as you can see below:

Even with SG&A expenses increasing, YETI’s revenue growth has outpaced costs, resulting in continued growth in net income and earnings per share over the last three years.

YETI has also further benefited investors by buying back roughly 6% of all outstanding shares in 2024 for roughly $200 million. The company still has $450 million available remaining under their current Share Repurchase Program.

YETI still has an excellent balance sheet, as you can see below:

The company still has enough current assets to cover all of their current liabilities. Furthermore, the company’s long-term debt balance has continued to decline while the company’s retained earnings balance has increased over the past year. Although YETI’s cash balance is down I’m fine with management using that cash to buy back shares and make appropriate acquisitions.

Expansion of a Beloved Brand

Thus far, I’ve discussed the numerous negatives associated with Trump’s tariffs and the potential ramifications. Yet, I am by nature an investor that likes to focus on the long-term, so I'd like to note what YETI has going in their favor and how the brand can continue to grow after the dust settles on this tariff drama.

First and foremost, YETI is a popular, beloved brand. This statement from CEO Matt Reintjes on the company’s latest earnings call illustrates the fact that YETI products are loved by consumers, “Our most recent US owner study completed in December underscores the strength of our brand. 2024 was the seventh consecutive year that 95% of YETI owner surveyed responded they have recommended YETI to peers, highlighting our incredible brand loyalty and a testament to our products. Moreover, the study shows that our consumers want us to be in the newer categories we're scaling in particular bags and travel.”

YETI also has far more social media followers than rivals Stanley and Hydro Flask, as you can see below:

As Reintjes noted above, YETI has numerous opportunities to continue to expand their product line. In 2024, YETI launched 24 new products and had a record number of color introductions. YETI has a particularly good opportunity to expand into travel and packs as the company can take advantage of their Mystery Ranch acquisition. Regarding this particular opportunity, here is what Reintjes had to say, “We have a big opportunity in front of us with a massive global addressable market across premium bags, packs and luggage and exciting launches that highlight our unique designs and capabilities, all leveraging our strong commercial engine and brand.”

Beyond packs and travel gear, YETI purchased some IP relating to a powered cooler system which could lead to future innovation for the company’s cooler portfolio and YETI will have opportunities in M&A as well. I think an opportunity such as Solo Stoves (DTC) could be a potential fit underneath YETI’s production umbrella, just as one example.

With product innovation, M&A or a combination of the two, YETI has numerous ways in the company can continue to grow in the years to come.

Valuation

YETI has a valuation grade of a "B-" at Seeking Alpha:

I still believe price to earnings is a good valuation metric for YETI, given it’s a profitable, established company. YETI's forward P/E ratio (Non-GAAP) is at 10.40 which is below the sector median of 13.32, and it's also far below YETI's five-year average. As can see below, YETI's current P/E ratio is at its lowest level in a year:

Looking at YETI's valuation another way, I have updated my reverse discounted cash flow model using the latest cash flow figures while keeping a discounted rate of 10% and a terminal rate of 3%. Based on current estimates, which likely aren’t taking Trump’s new tariffs into account, most analysts believe YETI's stock will continue to grow in the mid-single digits for the next several years. Taking a more bearish stance, if YETI can only grow at a rate of 3% which, I believe is conservative, then YETI is currently undervalued:

Conclusion

I remain a believer in YETI as the company is a beloved brand and one with diverse products. YETI has a nice omnichannel approach to their business and has opportunities to add new wholesale partners and expand their product lines.

YETI is likely undervalued at the moment, but I'm not in a rush to add, and I'll be waiting to hear if any potential deals are reached relating to Trump's tariffs. I'd also like to wait until management can provide more color on how the company has been working to move production out of China.

data.title: White Brook Capital Partners Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
White Brook Capital Partners as a commingled investment fund is winding down. Almost all positions have been sold and the account will sit in cash until it is dispersed to partners. White Brook Capital will remain and continue to manage separately managed accounts. So far this month, White Brook Capital Partners is down but has outperformed the midcap index as we continued to build cash.

Less than a week ago I was optimistic we had a uniquely positioned portfolio for the next few years and the likelihood of outperformance and positive performance was exceptional. The tariff announcement this past week, along with its calculation and implementation, however, confirmed what I thought was a low probability downside risk. It is clear that the economic environment is likely to be exceptionally unpredictable this year. Unless there’s a clear reversal in policy, in the next year, I think we’ll see something similar to the early days of Covid where this administration is willing to look past facts in order to operate in a parallel reality. Having done it once before, that’s a tough environment to invest in midcaps.

I have given everything I have to make White Brook Capital Partners a success, but the performance for the past 3 years has not met expectations. While I believe our relative performance would be positive this year, I now think there’s little chance to generate significant positive returns in a midcap strategy in the near term. White Brook Capital Partners is returning capital to investors.

I believe that, like during Covid, there will be opportunities to build generational wealth during the next several years. If we shorted stocks, we’d be able to match the volatility of the market and look to aggressively buy when individual stocks got exceptionally cheap. But White Brook Capital Partners does not short stocks or hold instruments that short stocks, to match against long positions and defray the volatility. As a long only partnership, we can only sit in cash to lower volatility. I’d recommend having a larger cash allocation for the next year, and if given the opportunity, I’d do that, earn yield, and wait for individual opportunities that were beyond compelling across market caps. But I do not have a crystal ball and I wanted to give each partner a chance to decide that for themselves.

The process of unwinding the portfolio is mostly complete. We sold a large percentage of the portfolio early on Thursday and the process will end in the next couple days. The funds will sit in cash until it can be disbursed to you. There is a final audit that needs to take place, as well. The administrator or I will reach out with specifics to return capital. It will hopefully be a relatively quick process.

White Brook Capital as a firm is not shutting down and will continue to manage separately managed accounts custodied at Charles Schwab or Interactive Brokers. I believe recent international trade moves are without precedent and short on rationale and therefore have acted quickly and defensively to protect your capital. Separately managed accounts have eliminated those positions that I believe are most sensitive to the difficult macroeconomic challenges that I believe are occurring and will continue to occur in the country as long as the administration stays on its current path. Market commentators talk about uncertainty in the marketplace, but the level of business uncertainty today hinders short and long term decision making across the board, whether it’s for a company making a new investment or just maintaining current efforts, whether to try to rewire the supply chain at significant cost, or sit tight, raise prices, and hope presidential passions change. It’s difficult for management teams to operate and make decisions today and even more so for investors to accurately divine the future cash flows and strategic position that those businesses may have with imperfect information.

Should prices become so compelling, or the investing environment begin to clarify, I would look to redeploy, but how and when that is, is unclear. I look forward to having individual conversations with clients as you have time. In the meantime cash will be a significant portion of the portfolio.

With the closure of the White Brook Capital Partners and what I believe will in time be an attractive environment, White Brook Capital will have three strategies moving forward.

White Brook Capital’s Ethical Index

Outlined in our last insights post, the Ethical Index is a diversified passive investment based on the S&P 500 (SP500, SPX) . It strips out vice and defense companies and those that have profited from the recent and continuing wars - leaving the index with 397 companies. It is more zealous in those aims, particularly the latter, than available options I have seen. For those who wish to strip out financial companies, that can be done as well. It has only started on April 1st and is performing in line with the S&P 500 as designed, through this downside volatility. The track record couldn’t be shorter, but there’s nothing in its construction that should result in significant divergence from the S&P 500 unless the above exclusions significantly outperform or underperform. I am happy to help clients directly or if you’d like to put me in touch with your Investment Advisor to find an arrangement that works - with them.

White Brook Capital Small Cap

The Small Cap Strategy targets small and microcap companies that we believe can grow strongly in a relatively short amount of time. Our first incubated account, started at the beginning of the year, is up over 100% this year, but that record is too short to have significant confidence in this extremely volatile strategy. These stocks are characterized by high analyzability, but low short term earnings confidence, and the opportunity for investment is measured in the hundreds of thousands and low millions of dollars in companies with market caps of less than 400 million. Each investment should be thought of as short term (1-2 years) with returns targeted at 100%, but with significant risk. We continue to only incubate this strategy.

White Brook Capital All Cap Discretionary

The All Cap Strategy targets all market caps and will hold a mix of ETFs (including the Ethical Index - at request), money market funds, and single stocks to generate returns where 60%-75% of the portfolio’s returns will be due to the allocation to “the market” or cash. The rest of the return profile will be due to single stock selection.

Unless a special situation arises - as it may with the small cap strategy - where a commingled fund is necessary - moving forward everything will be done in separately managed accounts custodied at Schwab or IB.

Again, I look forward to having individual conversations with clients as you have time. It is an honor to manage your investments, I thank you for your support, and will strive to continue to earn your trust.

Sincerely,

Basil F. Alsikafi | Portfolio Manager | White Brook Capital, LLC

All investments involve risk, including loss of principal. This document provides information not intended to meet objectives or suitability requirements of any specific individual. This information is provided for educational or discussion purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a solicitation to buy or sell securities. The information contained herein has been drawn from sources which we believe to be reliable; however, its accuracy or completeness is not guaranteed. This report is not to be construed as an offer, solicitation or recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities herein named. We may or may not continue to hold any of the securities mentioned. White Brook Capital LLC and/or their respective officers, directors, partners or employees may from time to time acquire, hold or sell securities named in this report. It should not be assumed that any of the securities transactions or holdings discussed were or will prove to be profitable, or that the investment decisions we make in the future will be profitable or will equal the investment performance of the securities discussed herein

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
